title: Doha Ceasefire Talks Emerge as US-Iran Strikes Enter Day Two, Israel-Lebanon Deal Rejected by Hezbollah, Europe's Heatwave Death Toll Tops 1,300
subhead: Washington and Tehran reportedly agree to halt attacks and resume negotiations in Doha, even as US bombs hit Iranian targets for a second day. Hezbollah rejects the Israel-Lebanon framework, calling it 'humiliating.' A historic heatwave kills over 1,300 across Europe. Venezuela's twin earthquakes leave tens of thousands missing.

sections.heading: US-Iran: Ceasefire Crumbles Into Day Two, But Doha Talks Reported
sections.body:
The US struck Iran for a second consecutive day, targeting sites near Sirik, Bandar-e Lengeh, and Qeshm Island, according to Al Jazeera. Iran's IRGC retaliated by launching missiles and drones at US military infrastructure in Kuwait and Bahrain; Bahrain activated air defences and sounded sirens, while Kuwait deployed air defence systems. Both Gulf states condemned the Iranian attacks, Al Jazeera reported.

A Panama-flagged cargo vessel was struck by a drone near the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, according to US Central Command. The incident prompted Trump to accuse Iran of breaching the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding. Iran in turn accused the US of violating the same deal first.

Yet by Sunday, Axios reported that Washington and Tehran had agreed to stop attacks and resume talks in Doha, Qatar. Iran simultaneously insisted on sole control of the Strait of Hormuz — the very issue that triggered the latest escalation. The IRGC claimed it had destroyed eight US military sites, a claim that could not be independently verified.

Aramco resumed crude oil loading at Ras Tanura on Friday after a nearly four-month suspension, according to Reuters. But on Sunday morning, an Aramco-owned helicopter crashed at Ras Tanura, killing all 14 Saudi citizens on board. The cause is under investigation.
sections.whyItMatters: The war is now in its 121st day and the Islamabad MoU — signed just two weeks ago — has already failed to hold. The Doha talks report is the first sign of a possible off-ramp, but Iran's insistence on Hormuz control is the core unresolved dispute. Every round of de-escalation so far has ended in renewed strikes.

sections.heading: Israel-Lebanon Framework Signed, Rejected by Hezbollah
sections.body:
Israel and Lebanon signed a US-brokered framework agreement in Washington on June 26, but the deal's viability is already in doubt. The agreement does not mandate Israeli withdrawal from the roughly 20% of Lebanese territory Israel currently occupies. Instead, it ties a 'progressive redeployment' to the verified disarmament of Hezbollah — a condition Hezbollah has flatly rejected.

Hezbollah chief Naim Qassem called the framework 'humiliating, shameful, and a surrender of sovereignty,' adding that linking withdrawal to disarmament 'crosses all red lines.' Hezbollah supporters burned tyres and blocked roads near Beirut's airport in protest. A Hezbollah parliamentarian said the deal could only be enforced through 'civil war,' Al Jazeera reported.

Lebanese President Joseph Aoun welcomed the deal as 'the first step on the path to restoring Lebanon's sovereignty.' Netanyahu framed it as allowing Israel to maintain its buffer zone until Hezbollah disarms. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio called it 'the beginning of the beginning' and announced $100m in humanitarian aid.

The framework directly contradicts the Islamabad MoU, which mandated an unconditional cessation of hostilities on all fronts, including Lebanon. Israel continued strikes on Lebanon even after the signing, with NNA reporting an Israeli drone strike in Nabatieh on Saturday. Over 4,192 people have been killed in Lebanon since the war on Iran began four months ago, according to Al Jazeera. More than 1.2 million have been displaced.
sections.whyItMatters: Two competing peace processes — the Islamabad MoU and the Washington framework — now contradict each other on the Lebanon question. Neither Israel nor Hezbollah has incentive to comply with the other's preferred mechanism. The likely trajectory is continued fighting under the cover of competing diplomatic documents.

sections.heading: Europe's Record Heatwave Kills Over 1,300
sections.body:
The WHO has linked approximately 1,300 deaths to the extreme heatwave sweeping Europe. France alone recorded over 1,000 excess deaths, according to AFP. Germany hit 41.7°C — an all-time national record. Poland set its own all-time record at 40.5°C. The Czech Republic also broke temperature records. The heat is now moving eastward across the continent.

AFP estimates that at least 191 million people across Europe are experiencing temperatures of 35°C or higher. The scale of mortality — over a thousand deaths in a single event — places this heatwave among the deadliest in European history, surpassing the 2003 event that killed an estimated 70,000 across the continent, though that toll accumulated over weeks rather than days.
sections.whyItMatters: The death toll is likely still rising as the heatwave shifts east. Beyond the immediate humanitarian toll, the event will intensify pressure on European governments on climate adaptation infrastructure and heat action plans — a politically loaded conversation after the recent European elections.

sections.heading: Venezuela: Twin Earthquakes Leave Tens of Thousands Missing
sections.body:
Two powerful earthquakes struck Venezuela, killing at least 1,450 people — though the figure is expected to rise. Satellite imagery published by Al Jazeera shows vast areas of destruction. Tens of thousands remain missing. Thirty-three survivors were pulled from rubble over the weekend, including two 11-year-old boys rescued separately.

Interim President Delcy Rodríguez faces the first major crisis of her tenure, with public anger mounting over government mismanagement under Venezuela's ruling socialist party, Al Jazeera reported. The earthquakes have compounded an existing humanitarian and economic crisis in a country already struggling with hyperinflation, mass migration, and infrastructure collapse.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale of destruction — tens of thousands missing — suggests the final death toll could be among the highest for an earthquake in the Western Hemisphere. The political fallout for Rodríguez could reshape Venezuela's internal politics at a moment when the opposition has been gaining ground.

sections.heading: Pakistan Strikes Afghan Border After Karachi Attack
sections.body:
Pakistan carried out air strikes and a ground operation along the Afghanistan border, killing 29 fighters, according to Information Minister Attaullah Tarar. Precision strikes hit targets in Paktia, Paktika, and Kunar provinces — all in eastern Afghanistan. The operation came one day after fighters armed with guns and explosives attacked the regional headquarters of the paramilitary Rangers in Karachi, killing three soldiers. Jamaat-ul-Ahrar, a breakaway faction of the Pakistan Taliban, claimed responsibility. The military identified one captured attacker as an Afghan national.

This is the second cross-border operation in less than three weeks, following a similar strike earlier in June. Pakistan and Afghanistan had observed about a month of relative calm after what Islamabad called an 'open war' between the neighbours. Multiple rounds of internationally mediated peace talks — including one hosted by China in April — have failed to secure a lasting ceasefire. Hundreds have been killed in cross-border fighting since February, Al Jazeera reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The Pakistan-Afghanistan border is now a persistent low-intensity conflict that dips in and out of international attention. Each escalation risks drawing in China as a mediator and complicating the broader regional security picture — especially as both countries' Taliban-affiliated armed groups operate with cross-border ties.

sections.heading: South Africa: Mass Repatriation of Foreign Nationals After Anti-Foreigner Violence
sections.body:
Over 6,900 Malawians have been repatriated from South Africa, with South African authorities processing 15,162 Malawian nationals for deportation, Al Jazeera reported. The exodus follows escalating anti-foreigner violence linked to South Africa's election period, during which xenophobic rhetoric has surged. Returnees described being attacked at night, their homes burned, their belongings looted. Many arrived back in Malawi with only donated clothes.

Malawi's government activated a 'comprehensive response plan' for an estimated 10,000 citizens in distress. Returnees told Al Jazeera that election cycles in South Africa consistently coincide with anti-foreigner attacks, suggesting the violence is not solely spontaneous but politically instrumentalised. Thousands more Malawians and other foreign nationals are expected to be repatriated in the coming weeks.
sections.whyItMatters: The systematic expulsion of over 15,000 people — and the destruction of their livelihoods — is a regional humanitarian crisis with economic ripple effects across southern Africa. It also raises questions about South Africa's commitment to the African Continental Free Trade Area's promise of free movement of people.

bottomLine: The US-Iran war entered its 121st day with both sides trading strikes even as Axios reported a Doha ceasefire understanding. Two competing peace processes — the Islamabad MoU and the Washington framework — now contradict each other on Lebanon, where Hezbollah has rejected a deal that ties Israeli withdrawal to its own disarmament. Europe's heatwave has killed over 1,300. Venezuela's earthquake toll is likely far higher than the current 1,450. Pakistan bombed Afghanistan again. South Africa is expelling tens of thousands of foreign nationals. No single conflict dominates — the world is running hot on every front at once.
